sections.title: weil — Causal Clauses
sections.explanation: The conjunction 'weil' (because) introduces a subordinate clause in which the conjugated verb moves to the END of the clause. This is called verb-final word order (Verbendstellung). The main clause is separated from the weil-clause by a comma. Example: 'Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich krank bin.' (I'm staying home because I'm sick.) If the weil-clause comes first, the main clause follows with inverted word order (verb before subject): 'Weil ich krank bin, bleibe ich zu Hause.' Note: In informal spoken German, 'weil' is sometimes (incorrectly) used with main-clause word order — avoid this in writing.
sections.tip: 'Weil' = verb goes to the end. Always. Even if there are multiple verbs: the conjugated verb is last. Compare with 'denn' (also 'because') which does NOT change word order — but 'denn' can only follow the main clause, while 'weil' clauses can go before or after.

examples.de: Ich lerne Deutsch, weil es interessant ist.
examples.en: I'm learning German because it's interesting.

examples.de: Er geht früh schlafen, weil er müde ist.
examples.en: He goes to bed early because he is tired.

examples.de: Weil es regnet, bleibe ich zu Hause.
examples.en: Because it's raining, I'm staying home.

examples.de: Sie isst kein Fleisch, weil sie Vegetarierin ist.
examples.en: She doesn't eat meat because she's a vegetarian.

examples.de: Ich kann nicht kommen, weil ich arbeiten muss.
examples.en: I can't come because I have to work. (modal at end)

exercises.prompt: Where does the verb go in a 'weil' clause?
options: To the end of the clause
exercises.explanation: In all German subordinate clauses, including 'weil' clauses, the conjugated verb moves to the very end of the clause.

exercises.prompt: Ich bin müde, weil ich lange ___ habe. (I'm tired because I worked long hours.) [arbeiten]
exercises.hint: Verb goes to the end of the weil-clause. Here it's perfect tense: haben + Partizip II (at the very end).

exercises.prompt: Which sentence is CORRECT?
options: Ich esse nicht, weil ich habe keinen Hunger.
Ich esse nicht, weil ich keinen Hunger habe.
Ich esse nicht, weil habe ich keinen Hunger.
Ich esse nicht, denn ich keinen Hunger habe.
exercises.explanation: 'weil' sends the verb to the end: 'weil ich keinen Hunger habe'. Option A has the verb in wrong position. Option C inverts subject/verb after weil. Option D uses 'denn' but with weil word order.

exercises.prompt: ___ er krank ist, geht er nicht zur Arbeit.
exercises.hint: The weil-clause comes first. The verb 'ist' is already at the end of the clause.

exercises.prompt: With a modal verb in a weil-clause, which is correct?
exercises.explanation: In a subordinate clause with a modal, the infinitive comes before the modal: 'weil ich arbeiten muss'. The modal is the conjugated verb and goes last.

exercises.prompt: Sie lacht, weil der Film sehr lustig ___. (She laughs because the film is very funny.)
exercises.hint: Conjugated verb goes to the end of the weil-clause.

exercises.prompt: What is the word order in the main clause when a weil-clause comes first? 'Weil es heiß ist, ___.'
exercises.explanation: When a subordinate clause precedes the main clause, the main clause uses inverted word order: verb before subject. 'Weil es heiß ist, trinke ich viel Wasser.'

exercises.prompt: Er kauft das Auto nicht, weil es zu teuer ___.
exercises.hint: Verb goes to the end. 'sein' conjugated for 'es' is...?

sections.title: dass — Complement Clauses
sections.explanation: 'dass' (that) introduces a complement clause — it often follows verbs of saying, thinking, believing, knowing, hoping, feeling: sagen (to say), denken (to think), glauben (to believe), wissen (to know), hoffen (to hope), fühlen (to feel), finden (to find/think). Like all subordinate clauses, the verb goes to the END. Example: 'Ich glaube, dass er kommt.' (I believe that he is coming.) Note: in English, 'that' can often be omitted, but 'dass' in German generally cannot be omitted — however, you can restructure without 'dass' by using an infinitive construction with 'zu' (covered separately).
sections.tip: After 'dass', verb goes to the end — just like 'weil'. Common dass-verbs to know: Ich glaube, dass... / Ich weiß, dass... / Ich hoffe, dass... / Er sagt, dass... / Ich finde, dass... The comma before 'dass' is always required in German.

examples.en: I believe that he is coming.

examples.de: Sie sagt, dass sie müde ist.
examples.en: She says that she is tired.

examples.de: Ich weiß, dass Deutsch schwer ist.
examples.en: I know that German is difficult.

examples.de: Er hofft, dass es morgen nicht regnet.
examples.en: He hopes that it won't rain tomorrow.

examples.de: Wir finden, dass diese Stadt sehr schön ist.
examples.en: We find that this city is very beautiful.
examples.highlight: dass diese Stadt sehr schön ist

exercises.prompt: Ich denke, ___ du Recht hast. (I think that you are right.)
exercises.explanation: 'dass' introduces a complement clause after 'denken' (to think). 'ob' would mean 'whether', 'weil' means 'because'.

exercises.prompt: Er weiß, dass sie jeden Tag Deutsch ___. (He knows that she studies German every day.) [lernen]
exercises.hint: Verb goes to the end of the 'dass'-clause. Conjugate 'lernen' for 'sie' (she).

exercises.prompt: Which sentence is INCORRECT?
options: Ich hoffe, dass es morgen schön ist.
Wir wissen, dass du hast Recht.
Er sagt, dass er keine Zeit hat.
exercises.explanation: 'dass du hast Recht' is wrong — in a dass-clause, the verb must go to the END: 'dass du Recht hast'.

exercises.prompt: Ich finde, dass dieser Film sehr ___ ist. (I find that this film is very boring.) [langweilig]
exercises.hint: The adjective (predicate) comes before 'ist' at the end of the dass-clause.

exercises.prompt: After which verb would you most naturally use a 'dass' clause?
exercises.explanation: 'hoffen' (to hope) is a mental/communicative verb that typically takes a 'dass' complement: 'Ich hoffe, dass...'

exercises.prompt: Sie sagt, dass sie morgen nicht ___ kann. (She says that she can't come tomorrow.) [kommen]
exercises.hint: With a modal in the dass-clause: infinitive before modal, modal at the very end.

exercises.prompt: Es ist wichtig, dass man ___ übt. (It is important that one practices daily.)
exercises.explanation: In the dass-clause, the conjugated verb goes to the end: 'dass man täglich übt'.

exercises.prompt: Ich bin froh, dass du ___ bist. (I'm glad that you are here.)
exercises.hint: The verb 'bist' is already at the end — what word meaning 'here' completes the sentence?

sections.title: wenn and ob — Conditional and Indirect Questions
sections.explanation: 'wenn' has two meanings: (1) 'when' for repeated/habitual events or future events: 'Wenn es regnet, bleibe ich zu Hause' (When it rains, I stay home). (2) 'if' for conditionals: 'Wenn ich Zeit hätte, würde ich kommen' (If I had time, I would come). 'ob' means 'whether/if' and introduces indirect yes/no questions: 'Ich weiß nicht, ob er kommt' (I don't know if/whether he's coming). Note: 'ob' is for indirect questions only — for direct yes/no questions, no 'ob' is used. Both 'wenn' and 'ob' follow the same rule: verb goes to the end.
sections.tip: Use 'wenn' for 'when' (habitual/future) and 'if' (conditional). Use 'ob' to turn a yes/no question into an indirect question: 'Kommt er?' → 'Ich frage mich, ob er kommt.' For indirect W-questions (who, what, where, why...), use the question word directly: 'Ich weiß nicht, warum er kommt.'

examples.de: Wenn es regnet, nehme ich einen Schirm.
examples.en: When it rains, I take an umbrella.

examples.de: Ich komme, wenn ich fertig bin.
Ich weiß nicht, ob er heute kommt.
examples.en: I don't know if/whether he's coming today.

examples.de: Sie fragt, ob wir mitkommen wollen.
examples.en: She asks whether we want to come along.

examples.de: Wenn du Hunger hast, machen wir etwas zu essen.
examples.en: If you're hungry, we'll make something to eat.

exercises.prompt: Ich frage mich, ___ er glücklich ist. (I wonder if/whether he is happy.)
exercises.explanation: 'ob' introduces indirect yes/no questions and means 'whether/if'. 'Ich frage mich, ob er glücklich ist.'

exercises.prompt: ___ du Zeit hast, können wir uns treffen. (If you have time, we can meet up.)
exercises.hint: Conditional 'if' introducing a subordinate clause with verb at the end.

exercises.prompt: Turn the question 'Kommt Maria morgen?' into an indirect question:
options: Ich weiß nicht, ob kommt Maria morgen.
Ich weiß nicht, ob Maria morgen kommt.
Ich weiß nicht, dass Maria morgen kommt.
Ich weiß nicht, wenn Maria morgen kommt.
exercises.explanation: 'Ich weiß nicht, ob Maria morgen kommt.' — 'ob' introduces the indirect yes/no question, verb goes to the end.

exercises.prompt: ___ ich jung war, spielte ich viel Fußball. (When I was young, I played a lot of soccer.)
exercises.hint: 'Als' is used for single past events; 'wenn' for repeated past or future events. Both are accepted here.

exercises.prompt: Er fragt, ob wir das Restaurant ___. (He asks whether we know the restaurant.) [kennen]
exercises.explanation: In the ob-clause, verb goes to the end: 'ob wir das Restaurant kennen'. The subject is 'wir', so 'kennen' (1st/3rd plural) is correct.

exercises.prompt: Ich weiß nicht, ___ sie morgen Zeit hat. (I don't know if she has time tomorrow.)
exercises.hint: Indirect yes/no question — use this conjunction.

exercises.prompt: Match each conjunction to its function:
Wenn das Wetter schön ___, gehen wir in den Park.
exercises.hint: Verb goes to the end of the 'wenn'-clause. 'sein' conjugated for 'das Wetter' (es-form).
